LAW AND MOTION 302, AMENDED MOTION OF INOAPPS, USA TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS AND DISMISS ALLIANTGROUP, LPS CROSS-COMPLAINT FOR LACK OF PERSONAL JURISDICTION IS GRANTED. ALLIANTGROUP HAS NOT SHOWN THAT THERE IS EITHER GENERAL OR SPECIFIC JURISDICTION OVER INNOAPPS. AS TO GENERAL JURISDICTION, ALLIANTGROUP HAS FAILED TO PROVIDE ANY EVIDENCE SHOWING THAT INOAPPS HAS SUBSTANTIAL, CONTINUOUS, AND SYSTEMATIC CONTACTS WITH CALIFORNIA. AS TO SPECIFIC JURISDICTION, THE ORACLE PARTNERNETWORK AGREEMENTS BETWEEN ORACLE AND INOAPPS DO NOT SHOW THE REQUIRED MINIMUM CONTACTS. UNLIKE THE FRANCHISE AGREEMENTS IN VONS COMPANIES INC. V. SEABEST FOODS, INC. (1996) 14 CAL.4TH 434, NOTHING IN THE ORACLE PARTNERNETWORK AGREEMENTS OR THE CONDUCT CARRIED OUT PER THOSE AGREEMENTS INDICATES THAT INOAPPS PURPOSEFULLY AVAILED ITSELF OF THE PRIVILEGE OF CONDUCTING BUSINESS IN CALIFORNIA. PREVAILING PARTY TO PREPARE A FORM OF ORDER AND TO COMPLY WITH CRC 3.1312. JUDGE: HAROLD KAHN; CLERK: CYNTHIA HERBERT; REPORTER: SHERRY SAWYER, CSR # 5976 =302/HK

DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED CROSS COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jan-04-2017 Overruled
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES - ATTY. MATT MARZULLO, TEL. NO. 1-713-350-3357 APPEARING FOR THE CROSS-COMPLAINANT;
ATTY. JAMES A. HUGHES OF THE SULLWOLD & HUGHES LAW FIRM, TEL. NO. 1-510-496-4616 APPEARING FOR THE CROSS-DEFENDANT.

RULING - A R G U E D; The Court adopted its tentative ruling. CROSS DEFENDANT ORACLE AMERICA, INC's DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED CROSS COMPLAINT - Cross-defendant Oracle America, Inc.'s demurrer to the first, second, and third causes of action in the second amended cross-complainant filed by alliantgroup, L.P. is overruled in its entirety. alliantgroup sufficiently alleges that the limitation of liability clause is inapplicable. The court cannot determine on this demurrer that the Oracle Cloud Services Agreement and Ordering Document is primarily to provide services, and not for the sale of software. Similar to the contract in RRX Industries, Inc. v. Lab-Con, Inc. (9th Cir.1985) 772 F.2d 543, it appears that the sale of software is paramount to any purported ancillary services. Because the UCC applies, alliantgroup may allege the contract's sole remedy provision of repair and refund "failed of its essential purpose" per Commercial Code 2719. alliantgroup sufficiently alleges the limitation of liability clause is inapplicable because the exclusive remedy failed of its essential purpose since alliantgroup was not required to pay under the agreement until 12 months after the contract's effective date and by that time it was clear that the software would not function as represented, Oracle was unable or unwilling to correct the software deficiencies in a commercially reasonable manner, and Oracle did not allow for the termination of the contract. (SACC 31-32.) Whether California common law permits the contractual limitation of damages is irrelevant given alliantgroup's allegations that the clause is inapplicable under the UCC. Order signed in open court. Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Reporter: Sherry Sawyer, CSR # 5976

Amended Notice Of Motion And Motion Of Inoapps, Usa To Quash Service Of Summons And Dismiss Alliantgroup, Lps Cross-Complaint For Lack Of Personal Jurisdiction
Off Calendar Jan-03-2017 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES - ATTY. MATT MARZULLO, TEL. NO. 1-713-350-3357 APPEARING FOR THE DEFENDANT/CROSS-COMPLAINANT;
ATTY. KENNETH E. KELLER OF THE KELLER SLOAN ROMAN HOLLAND LLP, TEL. NO. 1-415-249-8335 APPEARING FOR THE CROSS-DEFENDANT.

RULING - A R G U E D; The Court adopted its tentative ruling. Cross-defendant Innovative Applications, Inc.'s motion to quash service of summons and dismiss cross-complainant alliantgroup, L.P.'s cross-complaint for lack of personal jurisdiction is granted. alliantgroup has not shown that there is either general or specific jurisdiction over Innoapps. As to general jurisdiction, alliantgroup has failed to provide any evidence showing that Inoapps has substantial, continuous, and systematic contacts with California. As to specific jurisdiction, the Oracle Partnernetwork Agreements between Oracle and Inoapps do not show the required minimum contacts. Unlike the franchise agreements in Vons Companies Inc. v. Seabest Foods, Inc. (1996) 14 Cal.4th 434, nothing in the Oracle Partnernetwork Agreements or the conduct carried out per those agreements indicates that Inoapps purposefully availed itself of the privilege of conducting business in California. Prevailing party to prepare a form of order and to comply with CRC 3.1312. Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Reporter: Sherry Sawyer, CSR # 5976 =302/hk

DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED CROSS COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Oct-04-2016 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Attorney, James A. Hughes of Sullwold & Hughes (510.496.4616) appears as ocunsel for plaintiff/opposing party

Attorney, Matthew J. Marzullo of Alliantgroup LLP (713.350.3357) appears telephonically as counsel for defendant/cross-complainant/moving party

The cause is argued and submitted by both sides as set forth in the transcript of the certified shorthand reporter. The Court confirms the tentative ruling with modification.

CROSS DEFENDANT ORACLE AMERICA, INC.'s DEMURRER TO FIRST AMENDED CROSS COMPLAINT is SUSTAINED with 20 days leave to amend as to the first, second and third causes of action, OVERRULED as to the fourth, sixth and thirteenth causes of action and SUSTAINED without leave to amend as to the fifth cause of action for violation of section 17200. As to the first three causes of action the leave to amend is narrow, only if it can be done in good faith to either allege damages within the limitation of liability cause or to allege reasons why the limitation of liability clause is inapplicable. As to the first and second causes of action, cross-complainant alliantgroup, L.P. sufficiently alleges damages in paragraphs 31 and 36 of the amended cross-complaint. The correspondence between counsel is not subject to judicial notice. As to the third cause of action, alliantgroup sufficiently alleges bad faith conduct in connection with its obligation to provide services under the parties 'agreement in paragraphs 38-41of the amended cross-complaint. As to the fourth cause of action, alliantgroup sufficiently alleges that a benefit was conferred on Oracle in the form of knowledge about the functions and capabilities of the Fusion software. The fifth cause insufficiently alleges a loss that is subject to restitution under section 17200 as restitution has been defined in the section 17200 case law such as the Korea Supply case, i.e. there is nothing tangible that can be "restored" to alliantgroup. Since it does not appear that this deficiency can be remedied, alliantgroup is not given leave to amend to do so. As to the sixth cause of action, alliantgroup has alleged fraud with sufficient specificity to allow Oracle to understand fully the nature of the charges made. The particularity requirement in a fraud claim "necessitates pleading facts which show how, when, where, to whom, and by what means the representations were tendered." (Lazar v. Superior Court (1996) 12 Cal.4th 631, 645.) alliantgroup has pled that the alleged misrepresentations were made during several demonstrations in June 2014 at alliantgroup's headquarters in Houston, Texas by named Oracle employees about the viability of the Fusion software. While Oracle is correct that civil conspiracy is technically not a stand-alone cause of action and is instead a legal basis for imposing liability, no useful purpose is served by sustaining the demurrer to the thirteenth cause of action and then granting alliantgroup leave to allege conspiracy as part of its general allegations and/or as part of its sixth cause of action. The prevailing party shall submit a [proposed] written order that tracks the Court's ruling, verbatim and that complies with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: Jose Rios Merida; Reporter: Sherry L. Sawyer, CSR No. 5976, email: ssawyer@sftc.org. (D302)
